lead: When AI gives you something, an explanation is the part that turns it into words you can understand. It takes something tangled up inside a computer program, or in a big pile of data, or even inside your own head, and turns it into something you can say out loud and share. If you can't do that, you can't really see what is going on. And when you can't see what is going on, you can't be sure you should trust it.

Explanation is the layer that translates. It takes something complicated — from an AI, from a dataset, or from your own thinking — and turns it into a form you can share with others. Without it, AI output stays hidden from view, and you can't trust something you can't see into.

Explanation is the translation layer of augmented intelligence. It transforms complex representations — whether from a neural network, a dataset, or your own reasoning — into communicable form. Without it, AI output is opaque, and opaque output cannot be trusted.

Explanation is augmented intelligence's translation layer: it renders complex representations — from a neural network, a dataset, or your own reasoning — into communicable form. Opaque output cannot be trusted.
Explanation: the translation layer rendering complex representations (network, data, reasoning) communicable. Opaque output is untrustworthy.
enduring: A question worth carrying: what would it take to break this explanation? A real explanation has two qualities you can look for. First, it is hard to change: take one piece away and the whole thing falls apart. Second, it makes a claim that could be shown to be wrong if you went and checked. The "explanations" AI gives are often neither — nice-sounding words added on afterwards, easy to swap around, and rarely something you could prove false. So an explanation sounding good may not be reason enough to trust it. Whether it has parts you can actually check — and whether they hold up when you check them — is something you can decide for yourself.

A question worth carrying: what would it take to break this explanation? A genuine explanation is hard to change and can be proven wrong — take one part away and it breaks, and it commits to claims you could test. AI "explanations" are often nice-sounding words added afterwards: easy to change and rarely checkable. So reading well may not be reason enough to trust one. Whether it has parts that make it checkable — and whether they hold up — is yours to judge.

A question worth carrying: what would it take to break this explanation? A genuine explanation is hard to vary and falsifiable — change one part and it breaks, and it commits to claims that could be proven wrong. AI "explanations" are often plausible post-hoc text, easy to vary and rarely falsifiable. So an explanation reading well need not settle whether you trust it; the parts that make it checkable, and whether they hold up when you check them, are yours to weigh.

A question to carry: what would it take to break this explanation? A genuine explanation is hard to vary and falsifiable — alter one part and it breaks; it commits to claims that could be proven wrong. AI "explanations" are often plausible post-hoc text: easy to vary, rarely falsifiable. Readability need not settle trust; the checkable parts, and whether they hold, are yours to weigh.

One question to carry: what would break it? Genuine explanations are hard-to-vary and falsifiable; AI "explanations" are often easy-to-vary, unfalsifiable post-hoc text. Let fluency settle nothing — the checkable parts are yours to weigh.
core-problem: Here is the real problem. AI can make things that look like thinking, but the way it makes them is nothing like the way a person thinks. A language model does not first pick an answer and then explain why it picked it. It builds the answer and the explanation the exact same way — by guessing, one word at a time, what word should come next.

This is the core problem. AI can produce things that look like reasoning, but the process behind them is very different from how people reason. A language model doesn't "decide" on an answer and then explain its choice. It makes the answer and the explanation the same way — by predicting the next word, one at a time.

This is the core problem. AI systems can produce outputs that resemble reasoning, but the process that generated those outputs is fundamentally different from human reasoning. A language model does not "decide" to give an answer and then explain its decision. It generates the answer and the explanation through the same statistical process — predicting the next token in a sequence.

This is the core problem. AI outputs can resemble reasoning, but the generating process differs fundamentally from human reasoning. A language model doesn't "decide" an answer then explain it; it generates answer and explanation through the same statistical process — next-token prediction.
Core problem: AI outputs resemble reasoning but are produced unlike human reasoning. The model doesn't decide then justify — it generates answer and explanation by the same next-token prediction.
restaurant-analogy: Think about it like this. If someone asks why you picked a certain restaurant, you can walk back through what you were actually thinking: you felt like Thai food, this place had good reviews, and it was close enough to walk to. Each reason you give matches a real thing that went into your choice. AI explanations don't work this way. The "explanation" is something the AI puts together afterwards, and it may or may not match what the computer actually did to get the answer.

Here's the difference. When someone asks why you chose a restaurant, you can trace your real reasons: you wanted Thai food, it had good reviews, it was walkable. Each reason matches a real factor in your choice. AI explanations don't have this match. The "explanation" is built after the fact and may not reflect what actually produced the output.

When someone asks you why you chose a particular restaurant, you can trace your actual reasoning: you wanted Thai food, this place had good reviews, it was within walking distance. Each element of the explanation corresponds to an actual factor in your decision. AI explanations do not have this correspondence. The "explanation" is a post-hoc construction that may or may not reflect the computational process that produced the output.

Asked why you chose a restaurant, you can trace real reasoning: you wanted Thai, it had good reviews, it was walkable — each element maps to an actual factor in the decision. AI explanations lack this correspondence; the "explanation" is a post-hoc construction that may not reflect the computation that produced the output.
Human reasons map to actual decision factors (Thai, reviews, walkable). AI explanations lack this correspondence — post-hoc constructions that needn't reflect the producing computation.
deutsch-intro: A thinker named David Deutsch had an idea about explanations that are hard to change, and it gives us a clear way to tell a real explanation apart from empty noise. A real explanation has a few special things about it:
David Deutsch's idea of hard-to-change explanations gives us a clear way to tell a genuine explanation from noise. A genuine explanation has some specific qualities:
David Deutsch's concept of hard-to-vary explanations gives us a precise tool for distinguishing genuine explanation from noise. A genuine explanation has specific properties:
David Deutsch's hard-to-vary explanations give a precise tool for separating genuine explanation from noise. Genuine explanations share specific properties:
Deutsch's hard-to-vary criterion distinguishes genuine explanation from noise. Genuine explanations have specific properties:
the-test: The test: When AI hands you an explanation, ask yourself one simple question — could I take away or change any piece of this and still end up at the same conclusion? If the answer is yes, then the explanation is easy to change, and that means you shouldn't trust it. But if taking away any single piece makes the whole thing stop making sense, then you might have a real explanation, one that's worth building on.

The test: When AI gives you an explanation, ask — could I remove or change any part of this and still reach the same conclusion? If yes, the explanation is easy to change, and you shouldn't trust it. If removing any part breaks the reasoning, you may have a genuine explanation worth building on.

The test: When AI gives you an explanation, ask — can I remove or change any part of this and still reach the same conclusion? If yes, the explanation is easy to vary, and you should not trust it. If removing any element breaks the reasoning chain, you may have a genuine explanation worth building on.

The test: Ask whether you can remove or alter any part of the explanation and still reach the same conclusion. If yes, it's easy to vary — don't trust it. If removing any element breaks the reasoning chain, it may be genuine and worth building on. As David Deutsch put it, a good explanation is “hard to vary while still accounting for what it purports to account for” (Deutsch, 2011).

Test: Can any part be removed or changed with the conclusion intact? Yes → easy-to-vary, distrust. Removal breaks the chain → possibly genuine. Deutsch's formulation: a good explanation is “hard to vary while still accounting for what it purports to account for” (Deutsch, 2011).
black-box-intro: People often call modern AI systems black boxes — you put information in one end, you get results out the other, but you can't see anything that happens in the middle. This isn't just a way of speaking. A big neural network has billions of little parts, and the way they all work together is genuinely impossible to follow, even for the scientists who built it.

Modern AI systems are often called black boxes — you put data in, you get results out, but you can't see what happens in between. This isn't just a figure of speech. A large neural network has billions of parts whose interactions are truly opaque, even to the researchers who built it.

Modern AI systems are often described as black boxes — you put data in, you get results out, but you cannot see what happens in between. This is not a metaphor. A large neural network contains billions of parameters whose interactions are genuinely opaque, even to the researchers who built it.
Modern AI systems are often called black boxes: data in, results out, the middle unseen. Not a metaphor — a large network's billions of parameters interact opaquely, even to its builders.
Modern AI: black boxes — data in, results out, interior opaque even to builders. Literal: billions of parameters interacting inscrutably.
build-from-outside: For people who use AI in their work, this means you can't count on the AI to explain itself. You have to build the explanation yourself, from the outside. You do that by checking the answer against things you already know are true, by trying the same question different ways and comparing what comes back, and by confirming claims on your own. The explanation comes from what you do — not from the AI.

For people who use AI, this means you can't rely on the AI to explain itself. You have to build explanation from the outside — by testing the output against known facts, by comparing results across different prompts, and by verifying claims on your own. The explanation comes from your process, not from the model.

For practitioners, this means you cannot rely on the AI to explain itself. You must build explanation from the outside — by testing the output against known facts, by comparing results across different prompts, by verifying claims independently. The explanation comes from your process, not from the model.

For practitioners: don't rely on the AI to explain itself. Build explanation from the outside — test output against known facts, compare results across prompts, verify claims independently. Explanation comes from your process, not the model.
Practitioners can't rely on AI self-explanation. Build it externally: test against known facts, compare across prompts, verify independently. Explanation is your process, not the model's.
distillation-intro: Knowledge distillation means taking knowledge that is big and complicated and squeezing it down into something simpler that's easier to share. In the world of machine learning, it has a very exact meaning: training a small model to act like a big one. But the idea is useful in many more places than that.

Knowledge distillation is the process of squeezing complex knowledge down into simpler, easier-to-share forms. In machine learning, it specifically means training a smaller model to copy how a larger one behaves. But the idea applies much more broadly.

Knowledge distillation is the process of compressing complex knowledge into simpler, more communicable forms. In machine learning, it literally means training a smaller model to replicate the behaviour of a larger one. But the concept applies far more broadly.

Knowledge distillation compresses complex knowledge into simpler, more communicable forms. In machine learning it literally means training a smaller model to replicate a larger one's behaviour — but the concept applies far more broadly.
Knowledge distillation: compressing complex knowledge into simpler, communicable forms. In ML, literally training a small model to replicate a large one — but broadly applicable.
distillation-judgment: AI can help you with distillation, but it can't do it for you. A language model can shorten a paper into a summary, but it can't work out which findings matter most for your particular situation. It can describe a diagram, but it can't tell whether that diagram captures the relationships that really count or skips the one that matters most. Distillation needs your understanding of what's important. The AI gives you speed — you give the judgment.

AI can help with distillation, but it can't do it for you. A language model can summarise a paper, but it can't tell which findings matter most for your specific situation. It can describe a diagram, but it can't judge whether the diagram captures the essential relationships or misses the crucial one. Distillation needs your understanding of what matters — the AI provides speed, you provide judgment.

AI can assist with distillation, but it cannot do it for you. A language model can summarise a paper, but it cannot determine which findings are most important to your specific context. It can generate a diagram description, but it cannot judge whether the diagram captures the essential relationships or misses the crucial one. The distillation requires your understanding of what matters — the AI provides speed, you provide judgment.

AI assists distillation but cannot perform it for you. A model can summarise a paper but cannot determine which findings matter to your context; it can describe a diagram but cannot judge whether it captures the essential relationships or misses the crucial one. Distillation requires your understanding of what matters — AI provides speed, you provide judgment.

AI assists distillation, can't replace it: it summarises but can't rank findings for your context, describes diagrams but can't judge essential relationships. Distillation needs your judgment of what matters; AI supplies speed.
verifiable-intro: If you can't explain how the AI got to its answer, the next best thing you can do is make the answer something you can check. That means setting up the way you work so that whatever the AI claims gets tested against the real world before you decide to act on it.
If you can't explain how an AI reached its output, the next best thing is to make the output checkable — to set up your workflow so AI claims can be tested against reality before you act on them.

If you cannot explain how an AI reached its output, the next best thing is to make the output verifiable — to structure your workflow so that AI claims can be checked against reality before you act on them.
If you can't explain how an AI reached its output, the next best thing is to make it verifiable — structure your workflow so AI claims are checked against reality before you act.
Can't explain the output? Make it verifiable: structure the workflow to check AI claims against reality before acting.
the-principle: The principle: You don't need to understand how the AI works on the inside. What you need is to build a process around the AI that catches mistakes before they reach the real world. Explanation isn't only about understanding the model. It's about making your whole way of working clear enough to see into — and easy enough to fix when something goes wrong.

The principle: You don't need to understand how the AI works inside. You need to build a process around the AI that catches errors before they reach production. Explanation isn't just about understanding the model — it's about making the whole workflow transparent and correctable.

The principle: You do not need to understand how the AI works internally. You need to build a process around the AI that catches errors before they reach production. Explanation is not just about understanding the model — it is about making the entire workflow transparent and correctable.

The principle: You needn't understand the AI internally; you need a process around it that catches errors before production. Explanation isn't just understanding the model — it's making the entire workflow transparent and correctable.
Principle: Skip internal understanding; build a process that catches errors pre-production. Explanation = a transparent, correctable workflow, not model comprehension.
practice-intro: In the augmented intelligence way of thinking, explanation isn't a feature you sit around waiting for AI companies to make. It's a skill you build up yourself. Every single time you take something an AI produced and turn it into a form that another person — or you, later on — can understand and check, you are practising explanation.

In the augmented intelligence framework, explanation isn't a feature you wait for AI companies to build. It's a practice you develop. Every time you take an AI output and turn it into a form that someone else (or your future self) can understand and verify, you're practising explanation.

In the augmented intelligence framework, explanation is not a feature you wait for AI companies to build. It is a practice you develop. Every time you take an AI output and translate it into a form that someone else (or your future self) can understand and verify, you are practising explanation.

In the augmented intelligence framework, explanation isn't a feature you wait for AI companies to ship — it's a practice you develop. Each time you translate an AI output into a form someone else (or your future self) can understand and verify, you're practising explanation.
Explanation isn't a feature to await from AI vendors; it's a practice you develop — translating outputs into forms others (or future you) can understand and verify.
trap-text: Reasons that sound smooth and convincing are the cheapest thing a language model can make. They come out of the exact same one-word-at-a-time process that made the answer, so the model can dress up any answer with confident-sounding reasons — even an answer that's flat wrong. Smooth reasons are not proof that real thinking happened. The real test isn't whether the AI gave you reasons. It's whether those reasons are hard to change and could be proven wrong: can you pull out a piece without the conclusion changing, and could the claim turn out to be false? If not, what you have is just generated text dressed up in the costume of an explanation.

Reasons that read smoothly are the cheapest thing a language model produces — they come from the same next-word process as the answer, so the model can supply confident-sounding justification for any output, even a wrong one. Fluent reasons aren't evidence of real reasoning. The test isn't whether reasons were given but whether they're hard to change and can be proven wrong: can you remove a part without the conclusion changing, and could the claim turn out false? If not, you have generated text wearing the costume of an explanation.

Reasons that read fluently are the cheapest thing a language model produces — they come from the same next-token process as the answer, so the model can supply confident-sounding justification for any output, including a wrong one. Fluent reasons are not evidence of a real reasoning chain. The test is not whether reasons were given but whether they are hard to vary and falsifiable: can you remove a part without the conclusion changing, and could the claim turn out to be false? If not, you have generated text wearing the costume of an explanation.

Fluent reasons are the cheapest output a language model produces — same next-token process as the answer, so it can justify any output, including a wrong one. Fluency isn't evidence of a reasoning chain. The test isn't whether reasons were given but whether they're hard to vary and falsifiable: can a part be removed without changing the conclusion, and could the claim be false? If not, it's generated text costumed as explanation.

Fluent reasons are the cheapest model output — same next-token process as the answer, so it justifies anything, even errors. Fluency ≠ reasoning chain. Test: hard-to-vary and falsifiable, not merely given. Otherwise: generated text costumed as explanation.
